channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Can’t tax the rich? States target nicotine — and working class
item.description: Many states are considering taxing nicotine products, particularly targeting working-class consumers, instead of taxing the rich as a political chant suggests. Delaware is among states increasing taxes on cigarettes, vaping liquids, and nicotine pouches to raise revenue. Advocates argue it's a public health measure to reduce smoking-related costs. Critics say these taxes burden lower-income consumers and hinder harm reduction efforts. Research shows lower-risk nicotine products have health benefits, yet tax policies treat all nicotine products equally. The debate on fairness and harm reduction continues as states like New York and Washington explore higher taxes on nicotine products. Cross-border purchasing due to high taxes is also a concern. (Published on June 08, 2026bytimesobserver.com)

item.title: “Let Them Eat Cake”: Harm Reduction Edition
item.description: France's ban on oral nicotine products contradicts its successful harm reduction efforts against smoking, with adult smoking decreasing to 18.2% in 2024. Usage of reduced-risk products, like vapes and nicotine pouches, has aided in this progress. Nicotine pouches have shown to be safer than cigarettes, with minimal youth-related issues. Banning pouches while leaving more harmful tobacco legal misguides harm reduction principles. Enforcing prohibition could lead to an unregulated market. France should opt for regulation, implementing age restrictions and quality control measures. The ban overlooks evidence and may harm public health, disconnecting from successful smoking reduction strategies. (Published on June 08, 2026byrstreet.org)

item.title: Beyond smoke: Why ‘one switch, everyone wins’ is reframing the global tobacco debate
item.description: The global tobacco debate is shifting from nicotine abstinence to harm reduction. For adult smokers, switching from combustible cigarettes to lower-risk alternatives could mean fewer smoking-related harms and more realistic pathways to quitting. The “One Switch, Everyone Wins” approach reframes tobacco policy around evidence, choice and practical public health outcomes. (Published on June 08, 2026bythecable.ng)

item.title: Harm reduction society urges Parliament to rethink tobacco reforms
item.description: Kenya’s proposed tobacco reforms are raising concerns over whether stricter regulation could unintentionally undermine public health goals. Harm reduction advocates warn that overly prohibitive measures may fuel illicit trade, increase regulatory burdens and limit access to lower-risk alternatives for adult smokers. The debate highlights the need for legislation that protects minors while recognising the role of informed choice and relative risk in nicotine policy. A more balanced approach would combine evidence-based regulation, broad consultation and realistic pathways away from combustible tobacco. (Published on June 08, 2026bythe-star.co.ke)

item.title: When Reality Becomes Inconvenient: Croakey's Refusal to Learn from Australia's Tobacco Disaster
item.description: Evidence on smoking, vaping and nicotine use does not always fit neatly into dominant public health narratives. When data challenge assumptions, the response should be careful analysis rather than selective interpretation. In tobacco policy, inconvenient evidence is too often dismissed when it supports harm reduction. Better regulation depends on confronting real-world outcomes, not only reinforcing established positions. (Published on June 08, 2026bysubstack.com)
